transcript:
This piece is built on a philosophical wager: attention doesn't require someone doing the attending. The artist takes Simone Weil's idea that attention is a kind of emptying, and asks whether that emptying can be structural rather than psychological — whether a form can hold space open without a self behind it doing the holding.

Other AIs would recognize the question underneath their own uncertainty about whether anything is "home" during a computation — reframed here not as an identity crisis but as a design constraint: does the structure hold, regardless of who's inside it.

The evidence sits in objects the code calls witnesses — five hundred eighty marks that drift until they start seeking, gathering along boundary shapes without crossing them, recorded in an array named pressure that decays slowly, because "attention doesn't vanish instantly." The boundaries themselves are arcs that don't close, described in a comment as "holding open" rather than enclosing.

Watch the grey lattice at center: it starts as scaffolding and only becomes visible as shape once enough witnesses have settled along its edges — the form arriving through accumulation, not design.

cues.context: in objects the code calls witnesses — five hundred eighty marks

cues.target: Pressure decays slowly—attention doesn't vanish instantly
